edges.description: The arginine-rich basic tail changes the biophysical phase-separation behaviour of HMGB1.
edges.source: HMGB1 Frameshift and Acidic-to-Basic Tail Replacement

edges.description: Altered phase separation drives mispartitioning of HMGB1 into the nucleolus.
Nucleolar dysfunction perturbs the nucleolus's core role in ribosomal RNA biogenesis.
Impaired ribosome biogenesis during development is proposed to disrupt morphogenesis of skeletal and craniofacial structures.
Disrupted limb morphogenesis produces tibial aplasia/hypoplasia.
Disrupted limb morphogenesis produces preaxial polydactyly.
Disrupted forelimb morphogenesis produces radial hypoplasia.
Disrupted forelimb morphogenesis produces ulnar hypoplasia.
Disrupted craniofacial morphogenesis produces a depressed nasal bridge.
Disrupted craniofacial morphogenesis produces a short nose.
Disrupted craniofacial morphogenesis produces a narrow mouth.
Disrupted morphogenesis produces external genital hypoplasia.

edges.target: HMGB1 Frameshift and Acidic-to-Basic Tail Replacement

nodes.description: The mutant arginine-rich basic tail alters the liquid-liquid phase-separation behaviour of HMGB1, changing the condensates it forms and its partitioning between cellular compartments.

evidence.snippet: The mutant tail alters HMGB1 phase separation, enhances its partitioning into the nucleolus and causes nucleolar dysfunction.

nodes.description: Narrowing of the palpebral fissures, part of the characteristic craniofacial dysmorphism of the syndrome.

evidence.snippet: The patient had tibial aplasia, mirror image preaxial polydactyly involving her feet, brachyphalangy, genital hypoplasia as well as facial dysmorphism including telecanthus, blepharophimosis, a flat nasal bridge with a small nose and a small mouth.

nodes.description: Shortening of the digits (brachydactyly) and of the phalanges (brachyphalangy) affecting the hands and feet.

evidence.snippet: Clinical problems included brachydactyly of hands and feet, splaying of fingers and toes, preaxial polydactyly of feet, bilateral tibial aplasia, shortened radius and ulna, and characteristic facial dysmorphic signs.

nodes.description: Flattening/depression of the nasal bridge, a craniofacial feature of the syndrome.

evidence.snippet: The patient had tibial aplasia, mirror image preaxial polydactyly involving her feet, brachyphalangy, genital hypoplasia as well as facial dysmorphism including telecanthus, blepharophimosis, a flat nasal bridge with a small nose and a small mouth.

nodes.description: The cellular consequences of nucleolar dysfunction and impaired ribosome biogenesis manifest during development as disrupted morphogenesis of the limbs and craniofacial structures, producing the brachyphalangy, polydactyly, tibial aplasia, and craniofacial features that define the syndrome.

evidence.snippet: We discover de novo frameshift variants in HMGB1 that cause brachyphalangy, polydactyly and tibial aplasia syndrome, a rare complex malformation syndrome.

nodes.description: Hypoplasia of the external genitalia (e.g., micropenis and hypoplastic scrotum in males; absent labia minora and a small clitoris in females).

evidence.snippet: The patient had tibial aplasia, mirror image preaxial polydactyly involving her feet, brachyphalangy, genital hypoplasia as well as facial dysmorphism including telecanthus, blepharophimosis, a flat nasal bridge with a small nose and a small mouth.
We discover de novo frameshift variants in HMGB1 that cause brachyphalangy, polydactyly and tibial aplasia syndrome, a rare complex malformation syndrome.
The frameshifts replace the intrinsically disordered acidic tail of HMGB1 with an arginine-rich basic tail.

nodes.description: De novo heterozygous frameshift variants in the final exon of HMGB1 shift the reading frame so that the protein's intrinsically disordered, negatively charged acidic C-terminal tail is replaced by an arginine-rich basic tail. This is the primary molecular lesion of the syndrome.
nodes.id: HMGB1 Frameshift and Acidic-to-Basic Tail Replacement

evidence.snippet: We discover de novo frameshift variants in HMGB1 that cause brachyphalangy, polydactyly and tibial aplasia syndrome, a rare complex malformation syndrome.
The frameshifts replace the intrinsically disordered acidic tail of HMGB1 with an arginine-rich basic tail.

nodes.description: Shortening/underdevelopment of the radius, reported as part of the upper-limb long-bone involvement.

evidence.snippet: Clinical problems included brachydactyly of hands and feet, splaying of fingers and toes, preaxial polydactyly of feet, bilateral tibial aplasia, shortened radius and ulna, and characteristic facial dysmorphic signs.

nodes.description: Shortening/underdevelopment of the ulna, reported as part of the upper-limb long-bone involvement.

evidence.snippet: Clinical problems included brachydactyly of hands and feet, splaying of fingers and toes, preaxial polydactyly of feet, bilateral tibial aplasia, shortened radius and ulna, and characteristic facial dysmorphic signs.

nodes.description: Nucleolar dysfunction impairs ribosomal RNA biogenesis — the nucleolus's primary function — reducing ribosome production capacity in affected cells.

evidence.snippet: For 12 out of the 13 disease-associated variants tested, the mutation enhanced partitioning into the nucleolus, and several variants altered rRNA biogenesis.

nodes.description: A small mouth (microstomia), part of the craniofacial dysmorphism of the syndrome.

evidence.snippet: The patient had tibial aplasia, mirror image preaxial polydactyly involving her feet, brachyphalangy, genital hypoplasia as well as facial dysmorphism including telecanthus, blepharophimosis, a flat nasal bridge with a small nose and a small mouth.

nodes.description: Aberrant phase separation enhances partitioning of mutant HMGB1 into nucleolar condensates, where it mislocalizes, displaces the normal granular component, and disrupts nucleolar organization and function.

biological_processes: Mispartitioning of HMGB1 into the nucleolus

evidence.snippet: The mutant tail alters HMGB1 phase separation, enhances its partitioning into the nucleolus and causes nucleolar dysfunction.
For 12 out of the 13 disease-associated variants tested, the mutation enhanced partitioning into the nucleolus, and several variants altered rRNA biogenesis.

nodes.description: Preaxial (often mirror-image) polydactyly, most prominently affecting the feet.

evidence.snippet: Clinical problems included brachydactyly of hands and feet, splaying of fingers and toes, preaxial polydactyly of feet, bilateral tibial aplasia, shortened radius and ulna, and characteristic facial dysmorphic signs.

The patient had tibial aplasia, mirror image preaxial polydactyly involving her feet, brachyphalangy, genital hypoplasia as well as facial dysmorphism including telecanthus, blepharophimosis, a flat nasal bridge with a small nose and a small mouth.

nodes.description: A small/short nose, part of the craniofacial dysmorphism of the syndrome.

evidence.snippet: The patient had tibial aplasia, mirror image preaxial polydactyly involving her feet, brachyphalangy, genital hypoplasia as well as facial dysmorphism including telecanthus, blepharophimosis, a flat nasal bridge with a small nose and a small mouth.

nodes.description: Increased distance between the inner canthi of the eyes, a craniofacial feature of the syndrome.

evidence.snippet: The patient had tibial aplasia, mirror image preaxial polydactyly involving her feet, brachyphalangy, genital hypoplasia as well as facial dysmorphism including telecanthus, blepharophimosis, a flat nasal bridge with a small nose and a small mouth.

nodes.description: Absence or marked underdevelopment of the tibia, frequently bilateral, is a cardinal feature of the syndrome.

evidence.snippet: Clinical problems included brachydactyly of hands and feet, splaying of fingers and toes, preaxial polydactyly of feet, bilateral tibial aplasia, shortened radius and ulna, and characteristic facial dysmorphic signs.

The patient had tibial aplasia, mirror image preaxial polydactyly involving her feet, brachyphalangy, genital hypoplasia as well as facial dysmorphism including telecanthus, blepharophimosis, a flat nasal bridge with a small nose and a small mouth.
